notationMapping.description: Single source of truth for ALL notation mappings in the Songbook Pipeline. Edit this file to change how Indian sargam, internal tokens, and Western notes relate to each other. All Python scripts should read from this file instead of hardcoding mappings.

notes: WORD SYSTEM (canonical display format used in song JSON files):
Shuddh swar: Sa Re Ga ma Pa Dha Ni
Low octave: pa dha ni (lowercase words)
High octave: Sa' Re' Ga' Ma' ma' Pa' (quote suffix)
Komal swar: Re(k) Ga(k) Dha(k) Ni(k)
Shuddh swar: S R G m P D N
Low octave: p d n (lowercase letters)
High octave: S' R' G' etc (quote suffix)
Komal swar: D(k) N(k) R(k) G(k)
Natural: C D E F G A B
Flat: Db Eb Ab Bb (komal swar)
Low octave: lowercase (e.g., g a b)
High octave suffix: ' (e.g., C' D' E')

letter_to_word._comment: Convert single-letter notation (scraped) to word notation (display). Used by scrape_notation_url.py and convert_notation.py.

word_to_token._comment: Convert word notation to internal tokens for processing/MusicXML generation.

word_to_western_display._comment: Convert word notation directly to Western display strings. Used by add_western_notes.py, generate_western_json.py, etc.

letter_to_western_display._comment: Convert single-letter notation directly to Western display strings. Legacy support for scripts that still use letter notation.

token_to_western._comment: Convert internal tokens to Western MusicXML format (step + alter). Used by build_songbook.py for MusicXML generation.

word_canon._comment: Canonical casing for word-form swar. Used to normalize SA->Sa, RE->Re, etc.

lines.lyrics: “Tu Hi Re” from Bombay is one of those songs that gets straight to your soul. With A.R. Rahman’s haunting composition, Hariharan’s soulful voice, and Manisha Koirala’s expressive performance, it’s pure magic. But like most beautiful things, getting it right wasn’t easy.

Manisha once shared how shooting for this iconic song wasn’t as romantic as it looked on screen. Imagine standing on sharp, slippery rocks with waves crashing all around you — sounds scary, right? Well, that’s exactly what she did. And if that wasn’t enough, the next scene had her running through a jungle full of leeches! Apparently, every step she took came with a fresh batch of those little bloodsuckers clinging to her legs. The crew tried everything from salt to boots, but nature had its own plans.

The stunning Bekal Fort in Kerala, where the song was filmed, added another layer of grandeur. Overlooking the Arabian Sea, its ancient stone walls stood in stark contrast to the tender emotions of the song. It’s no wonder the location became a tourist hotspot after the movie’s release.

Here’s another lesser-known nugget — K.S. Chitra originally recorded the Hindi version of “Tu Hi Re.” But the music label reportedly wanted a more familiar North Indian voice, leading to Kavita Krishnamurthy stepping in. Chitra, being the gracious artist she is, had nothing but praise for Rahman, who personally informed her about the switch. It’s moments like these that remind you how much thought and care go into creating the music we love.
Next time you listen to “Tu Hi Re,” just think about the crashing waves, the relentless leeches, and the resilience of the team behind it. Makes the song feel even more special, doesn’t it?
